title: IELTS Speaking: 5 Principles That Actually Matter
subtitle: Master the strategies that beat memorised perfection

lessons.title: Test Overview
lessons.liz_intro: Welcome to the Speaking guide. The test is short — 11 to 14 minutes — but every part demands a different mindset. Let's walk through the structure first.
lessons.liz_outro: Now you know the structure and what's being marked. Next: the five principles that decide your score.

slides.title: IELTS Speaking Crash Course
slides.subtitle: Master the strategies that truly matter

items.title: The complete test takes 11–14 minutes and consists of three distinct parts, each requiring a different approach and strategy.

cards.title: Part 1 — 4–5 minutes
cards.body: Simple questions about YOU. Work and studies, hometown and family, hobbies and interests.

cards.title: Part 2 — up to 4 minutes total
cards.body: **1 minute prep + 1–2 minutes speaking + 1 minute rounding-up questions.** You get a topic card and 1 minute to make notes; then you speak alone for **1 to 2 minutes** (don't stop early — aim for the full 2). The examiner asks 1–2 short follow-ups after.

cards.title: Part 3 — 4–5 minutes
cards.body: Abstract discussion questions. Deeper thinking, complex ideas, critical analysis.

slides.title: Your Score Breaks Down Like This
slides.intro: Your performance is evaluated equally across four key areas. Understanding these criteria is essential for targeted improvement.

cards.title: 1. Fluency & Coherence
cards.body: How smoothly and logically you speak.

cards.title: 2. Vocabulary
cards.body: Range and accuracy of words used.

cards.title: 3. Grammar
cards.body: Variety and correctness of grammar.

cards.title: 4. Pronunciation
cards.body: Clarity and natural speech patterns.

lessons.title: Principle #1 — Different Strategy for Each Part
lessons.liz_intro: Most candidates use the same approach for all three parts. That's why their score plateaus. Each part needs its own playbook.
lessons.liz_outro: Different parts, different rules. Now we get to the most counter-intuitive principle of all.

items.title: Each part of the IELTS Speaking test demands a unique approach. What works in Part 1 won't serve you well in Part 3.

blocks.text: Part 1 is designed to put you at ease. Keep your answers natural, brief, and focused on personal information.

cards.title: Answer Length
cards.body: 2–3 sentences per answer.

cards.title: Content Focus
cards.body: Questions about YOU and your life.

cards.title: Approach
cards.body: Answer **directly**, keep it **brief**, and **add one reason or example**. No prepared stories, no overthinking — but don't ramble either. Two-to-three sentences per answer is the sweet spot.

blocks.text: I'm a software engineer. I work for a tech company developing mobile apps. I've been doing it for about three years now.
Notice how this answer is direct, relevant, and conversational — exactly what Part 1 requires.

slides.title: Part 2 — Your 2-Minute Speech
slides.intro: Many students misunderstand Part 2. Here's what actually matters versus the common misconception.

columns.label: Common Mistake
items: Must cover every bullet point on the card
Stop the moment you've answered all four

columns.label: Actual Requirement
items: Talk about the TOPIC at the top
Keep your speech flowing for 2 minutes
Tell stories, give specific examples — show, don't just tell

slides.title: Show Depth of Thinking
slides.intro: Part 3 tests your ability to discuss abstract ideas. Use this simple three-part structure for every answer.

slides.eyebrow: DECISION TREE — WHICH PART, WHICH MOVE
slides.title: When the examiner speaks, run THIS check first.
slides.body:
**Step 1 — Which part are you in?**

• *4–5 minutes in, short personal questions ('What's your job?', 'Do you like cooking?')* → **Part 1** → Answer in **2–3 sentences**. Direct + one reason. No stories.

• *Examiner hands you a cue card with bullet points* → **Part 2** → Use **1 minute** to plan, then speak for **2 minutes**. Tell a story. Don't worry about hitting every bullet — stay on topic.

• *Abstract questions starting with 'Why…?', 'How…?', 'Do you think…?'* → **Part 3** → Use **Direct Answer → Explanation → Example**. Speak for 30–45 seconds per answer.

**Step 2 — When in doubt, ask yourself:** *'Is this about me, or about ideas?'* About me = Part 1 style (short, personal). About ideas = Part 3 style (longer, opinion + example).

cards.title: Direct Answer
cards.body: People travel to experience something different from their daily routine.

cards.title: Explanation
cards.body: Everything feels fresh and exciting when you're in a new place — the food, the culture, even the way people interact.

cards.title: Example
cards.body: When I visited Japan, even ordering coffee became an adventure because of the language barrier and different customs.

lessons.title: Principle #2 — Be Natural, Not Perfect
lessons.liz_intro: This is the principle most students refuse to believe. Trying to sound perfect is exactly what makes you sound worse.
lessons.liz_outro: Authenticity beats rehearsed perfection. Now: why fluency matters more than accuracy.

items.title: Authenticity trumps perfection every time. The examiner values genuine communication over rehearsed performances.

slides.title: What High Scorers Actually Sound Like

blocks.text: Watch how high-scoring students speak naturally and confidently, making small mistakes but maintaining excellent fluency and coherence throughout.

**Notice:** Even Band 9 students pause, correct themselves occasionally, and don't use complex vocabulary in every sentence. What matters is the overall impression of natural, effortless communication.
This is what examiners are trained to detect: overly perfect responses that lack natural speech patterns and authentic interaction.
**Warning Signs:** unnatural vocabulary choices, zero hesitation, lack of self-correction, and responses that don't quite match the question being asked.

**What to do instead — three concrete habits:** *(1)* leave **natural pauses** to think ("Hmm, let me think...", "That's an interesting one"); *(2)* **self-correct openly** when you misspeak ("...I goes — I mean, I went...") — examiners reward this, it's a Band 7 marker; *(3)* let your voice **respond** to the question, not recite at it. Look at the examiner. React to what they say.

items.title: Liz can interview you in the same conversational style examiners use — then play back what sounded natural and what sounded rehearsed.

lessons.title: Principle #3 — Fluency Beats Perfection
lessons.liz_intro: If you have to choose between speaking smoothly with a few mistakes or stopping every sentence to be perfect — choose smooth. Every time.
lessons.liz_outro: Fluency over accuracy. Now let's tackle the vocabulary myth that's wrecking thousands of speaking scores.

items.title: The ability to keep speaking smoothly is more valuable than grammatically perfect but hesitant speech.

**Where this matters most:** Part 2 (long turn) — you have to keep speaking for two minutes; pausing to fix grammar mid-sentence kills your fluency score. In Part 1 short answers, accuracy still matters because each answer is so short there's no flow to protect.

slides.title: Part 2 = the 'long turn'.
slides.body:
**Long turn** is the official IELTS name for **Part 2** of the speaking test:

• You receive a cue card with a topic.
• You get **1 minute** to prepare and make notes.
• You then speak **alone** — no examiner questions, no help — for **1 to 2 minutes**.

It's called a 'long turn' because *you* hold the floor the whole time. This is where most candidates panic and stop talking. Fluency strategies (linking words, filler phrases, mini-stories) exist to help you fill those 2 minutes without freezing.

cards.title: Keep flowing
cards.body: Their speech moves naturally — no abrupt full stops.

cards.title: Use natural pauses
cards.body: Only pause to think, not to search for the "perfect" word.

cards.title: Connect smoothly
cards.body: Linking words bridge ideas instead of breaking them.

cards.title: Move past mistakes
cards.body: Accept small slips and keep going.

blocks.text: **Remember:** native speakers make grammatical mistakes constantly. The examiner expects some errors — they're looking for natural communication ability.

lessons.title: Principle #4 — Real Vocabulary (Not Lists)
lessons.liz_intro: Vocabulary lists are the comfort food of IELTS prep — easy to teach, easy to sell, and almost completely useless. Here's what works instead.
lessons.liz_outro: Words live in context. Lists die in your head. Now: the final principle, and the hardest one to do alone.

items.title: Stop memorising vocabulary lists. Start learning words the way native speakers do.

**Where this matters most:** Part 3 (discussion) — abstract topics reward natural **collocations** (*words that naturally go together: "discuss thoroughly" not "discuss deeply"; "make a decision" not "do a decision"*) and topic-specific vocab. In Part 1, formal words like 'magnificent' for 'a nice weekend' will *lower* your score for inappropriate register.

blocks.text: These popular study methods seem logical but often lead to unnatural language use and lower scores.

cards.title: ✗ "Band 9 Vocabulary Lists"
cards.body: Decontextualised words you'll misuse.

cards.title: ✗ Idiom in every sentence
cards.body: Sounds forced and unnatural.

cards.title: ✗ Never repeating words
cards.body: Creates awkward, unnatural speech.

cards.title: ✗ Academic word lists for *Part 1*
cards.body: Wrong register for casual chat ("What's your favourite food?" doesn't need 'gastronomically'). **But:** Part 3 abstract questions *do* reward precise, sometimes academic-feeling vocabulary used in context — "juxtapose", "paradigm" are fine if they fit the idea. The fault isn't the word — it's the register mismatch.

cards.title: ✗ Topic-based vocabulary lists
cards.body: Leads to memorised, scripted answers.

blocks.text: **Why these methods persist:** easy to teach ≠ actually works. They're popular because they're simple to create and sell, not because they produce results.

slides.eyebrow: REGISTER = MATCHING THE WORD TO THE MOMENT
slides.title: Same idea, different parts. Wrong register = lower score.
slides.body:
**Register** is how formal or casual your word choice is. IELTS examiners *reward* matching register and *penalise* mismatch. Same idea, different register:

• *'I had a good weekend'* — fine for Part 1 (casual chat). ✅
• *'I had a magnificent weekend'* — wrong register for Part 1 — sounds memorised. ❌
• *'It's a really popular sport here'* — fine for Part 1. ✅
• *'It's an extraordinarily prevalent recreational pursuit'* — wrong register for Part 1. ❌
• *'In contemporary society, individuals increasingly prioritise…'* — fine for Part 3 (abstract discussion). ✅
• *'Loads of people these days care a lot about…'* — too casual for Part 3. ❌

**Rule of thumb:** Part 1 = how you'd talk to a colleague at lunch. Part 3 = how you'd talk on a podcast about a serious topic. Same person, different setting.

slides.title: Effective Vocabulary Strategies
slides.intro: Evidence-based vocabulary acquisition strategies that mirror how successful language learners naturally develop their skills.

cards.title: 01. Learn Through Reading & Listening
cards.body: Encounter words in natural contexts repeatedly.

cards.title: 02. Master Pronunciation + Collocations + Usage
cards.body: Know how to say it, what goes with it, and when to use it.

cards.title: 03. Use Words You Understand 100%
cards.body: Confidence matters more than complexity.

cards.title: 04. Practise in Your Own Sentences
cards.body: Make the vocabulary personally meaningful.

slides.title: Build Vocabulary the Way That Actually Works
slides.intro: Skip the dead-end word lists. Use the surfaces inside IELTS Ace that grow your vocabulary in context.

cards.title: Vocabulary Tracker
cards.body: Save real phrases as you encounter them in Liz's lessons, with collocations and usage notes already attached.

cards.title: Liz Speaking Practice
cards.body: Liz reuses your saved phrases in conversation so you actually use them — not just recognise them.

cards.title: Listening + Reading Question Bank
cards.body: Real Cambridge passages give you words in the natural contexts that make them stick.

cards.title: Mistake Analysis
cards.body: Every word you misuse in writing or speaking is logged so you can practise it back into your active vocabulary.

lessons.title: Principle #5 — Get Professional Feedback
lessons.liz_intro: You can't fix what you can't hear. Most candidates have no idea which 3–4 errors are holding them back. That's where targeted feedback wins.
lessons.liz_outro: Five principles. Now let's bring them all together.

items.title: Self-study can only take you so far. Professional guidance identifies blind spots and accelerates improvement.

slides.eyebrow: NO TEACHER YET? DO THIS THIS WEEK.
slides.title: The 4-step self-feedback workflow.
slides.body:
Until you have a teacher (or Liz Live), this is how to find your own 3–4 repeating mistakes:

**(1)** Pick a Part 2 cue card. Record yourself for **2 minutes** on your phone — no script.  
**(2)** Listen back and **transcribe it word-for-word** (yes, every 'um', every restart). This is uncomfortable; do it anyway.  
**(3)** Highlight every error: grammar slip, wrong word, repeated filler. Look for **patterns** — the same mistake 3+ times.  
**(4)** Pick the **top 3 patterns**. Drill *only* those for one week. Then re-record the same cue card and compare.

**Why this works:** Band 6.5 → 7 is usually about killing 3–4 repeating errors. You can't fix what you haven't *seen* in writing.

slides.title: Three Common Mistakes
slides.intro: These errors can severely limit your score, even if other aspects of your speaking are strong.

steps.title: Common Grammar Errors
steps.body: Most students have no idea they are making the same errors over and over again.

steps.title: Inappropriate Vocabulary
steps.body: Using "magnificent" or "splendid" in casual conversation sounds unnatural and actually hurts your score.

steps.title: Not Answering the Question
steps.body: Showing off vocabulary instead of addressing what was asked demonstrates poor communication skills.

slides.title: You Don't Know YOUR Specific Mistakes

blocks.text: And that's what's holding you back from your target band score.
**The truth about band improvement:** Band 6.5 → Band 7 typically requires fixing just **3–4 consistent errors**. But which 3–4 errors are holding YOU back? You need someone trained to identify them.
Self-assessment has limits. Professional feedback reveals patterns you can't see on your own.

slides.title: Practise With Liz — Your Always-On Speaking Coach
slides.intro: Real-time speaking practice with Liz, plus a Sonnet evaluation that names the 3–4 errors actually holding you back.

cards.title: Liz Live — Real-Time Speaking
cards.body: Voice conversation with Liz that mirrors a real examiner: Part 1, Part 2 cue card, and Part 3 follow-ups.

cards.title: Identify YOUR 3–4 Errors
cards.body: Every session is evaluated against the four marking criteria so the patterns surface — not vague advice.

cards.title: Targeted Feedback
cards.body: Specific phrases to fix, specific habits to drop. No generic tips.

cards.title: Track Progress Over Time
cards.body: Your speaking history is saved so you can see fluency, vocabulary range and pronunciation improve session by session.

lessons.title: Recap & Next Steps
lessons.liz_intro: Five principles. That's the entire system.
lessons.liz_outro: Now you know what works. The next step is using it.

slides.title: The 5 Principles
slides.intro: These fundamental principles form the foundation of successful IELTS Speaking performance.

steps.title: Different Strategy for Each Part
steps.body: Part 1 needs brevity, Part 2 needs flow, Part 3 needs depth.

steps.title: Be Natural, Not Perfect
steps.body: Authenticity and genuine communication beat rehearsed perfection.

steps.title: Fluency Beats Perfection
steps.body: Keep speaking smoothly — minor errors are acceptable.

steps.title: Learn Vocabulary Through Context
steps.body: Real reading and listening, not memorised lists.

steps.title: Get Professional Feedback
steps.body: You can't identify your own blind spots.

slides.title: Quick Check: Speaking Principles
slides.intro: Five quick questions to lock in the principles before you walk into the test room.

items.stem: What's the right approach to Part 1 vs. Part 3 answers?

options.text: Same length and depth — examiners want consistency.
Part 1: short and natural; Part 3: longer, opinion-led, with reasoning.
Memorise template answers and reuse them across all three parts.
Part 1: long and detailed; Part 3: short to save time.

items.explanation: Part 1 rewards brevity and natural rhythm; Part 3 rewards depth, opinion, and reasoning. Different parts demand different strategies.

items.stem: A candidate has memorised a polished 'family' answer and recites it word-for-word. The examiner is most likely to:

options.text: Reward them for fluency and vocabulary.
Detect rehearsed language and lower the score.
Skip the rest of Part 1 to save time.
Ask them to repeat it more slowly.

items.explanation: Examiners are trained to spot memorised responses. Authentic, natural answers — even slightly imperfect — score better than rehearsed perfection.

items.stem: Complete the principle: '________ beats perfection.' (one word)
items.explanation: Keep speaking. Smooth delivery with minor grammar slips outscores hesitant, perfectly-formed sentences.

items.stem: What's the best way to grow your real Speaking vocabulary?

options.text: Read and listen to authentic English so words stick in context.
Use the longest synonym you can think of in every answer.
Translate Turkish idioms directly into English.

items.explanation: Vocabulary that lives in context — from real reading and listening — sounds natural. Memorised phrases sound forced and often get misused.

items.stem: Why can't you reliably self-assess your own speaking?

options.text: Your accent always sounds bad to you.
Recording yourself is technically too hard.
You can't hear your own consistent 3–4 errors — it takes a trained ear or calibrated evaluator.
Self-assessment is fine — professional feedback is overrated.

items.explanation: Band 6.5 → 7 usually requires fixing just 3–4 consistent errors — but you can't see your own blind spots. Professional or examiner-calibrated feedback names them.

blocks.text: You've got the principles. Now apply them in real conversation — Liz interviews you across all three parts and points out exactly which of the five principles needs work.

cards.title: Part 1, 2 & 3 in one session
cards.body: A full mock that mirrors the real test format.

cards.title: Sonnet-graded feedback
cards.body: Calibrated to IELTS examiner descriptors — not inflated scores.

cards.title: Saved to your dashboard
cards.body: Watch fluency, vocabulary and pronunciation improve session by session.
